summary: Strong Roses & Ship of Fools; solid second set despite mixed vibe
blurb: The show delivers standout performances of It Must Have Been The Roses, To Lay Me Down and Ship Of Fools, with a lively Touch of Grey segue into the second set. Jerry’s guitar shines early but his voice is hoarse at the start; Brent adds subtle keyboard texture. Overall the band stays cohesive though the mix varies across recordings.
review:
April 11, 1988 at Detroit’s Joe Louis Arena was an unassuming night that still managed a few memorable peaks. The first set built momentum with It Must Have Been The Roses and To Lay Me Down, both highlighted for their emotive delivery, while Jerry’s guitar tone on Touch of Grey in the second set earned particular praise. Brent’s understated keyboard phrasing threaded through the early songs, and the dual‑drummer birthday tribute added a playful burst of energy before the band launched into Ship Of Fools, The Wheel and a soulful Stella Blue. Vocal moments were uneven – Jerry sounded hoarse at the outset and Bob’s performance on Watchtower drew criticism – but the group recovered for a warm closing with Brokedown Palace. Recording-wise, the Miller soundboard (gd1988-04-11.sbd.miller.91243.sbeok.flac16) offers clear, balanced capture, whereas another SBD suffers from an imbalanced mix that muddies the overall cohesion.

Fans looking for a solid snapshot of this era should gravitate to the Miller SBD: it presents the band’s strengths without the distracting technical flaws found on other tapes. While not a landmark show, the set delivers enough strong moments – especially Roses, Ship Of Fools and the birthday drum duet – to make it worth a listen for any Deadhead catalog.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: All three recordings report mixed sentiment: positive notes on Roses, Touch of Grey and the birthday drum tribute are balanced by criticisms of hoarse vocals, an imbalanced mix and a generally shaky performance. The net effect is a modest positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Good soundboard clarity, balanced mix and captures the highlighted performances without major drop‑outs.

key_highlights: Strong renditions of It Must Have Been The Roses, To Lay Me Down, and Ship Of Fools.
Energetic second set featuring Touch of Grey, Man Smart Woman Smarter, and a lively birthday drum duet.
Brent Mydland's subtle keyboard phrasing adds texture early in the show.
Generally good recording quality; one SBD suffers from an imbalanced mix.
song_highlights: It Must Have Been The Roses

band_performance.Jerry: Guitar tone and energy are praised, especially on Touch; early vocals sound hoarse but improve by the final chorus of Ship Of Fools.
band_performance.Bob: Vocal performance on Watchtower criticized as 'stinkin’ up the place', yet his birthday serenade is described as sweet and silly.
band_performance.Brent: Subtle keyboard phrasing receives a positive nod in the early part of the show.
band_performance.Mickey: Percussion gets strong praise for its surround‑sound presence; both drummers are celebrated on their birthdays.

must_listen_sequences: It Must Have Been The Roses
